title: Methods
subtitle: Build your own reusable commands and call them whenever you need.
goal: Write static methods that take parameters and return values, call them from main, and use method overloading to reduce repetition.

blocks.heading: What is a method? 🧰
blocks.html:
A method is a reusable block of code with a name. You write the steps once, give them a name, and then you can run those steps any time by calling the name — no copy-paste needed.

You have actually been using a method all along: main is a method! Today you'll write your own helpers next to main and call them from inside it.

blocks.heading: Making a method that returns a number
blocks.html:
Here is the shape of a simple method:

static int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }

Reading it left to right: static means it belongs to the class (so main can call it directly), int is the return type (the kind of answer it hands back), add is the name, and (int a, int b) are the parameters it needs.

blocks.caption: A method that adds two numbers
blocks.explain: add(int a, int b) takes two numbers and returns their sum. In main we call add(5, 3), and the result (8) comes back to replace the call. We can call it as many times as we like.

blocks.html: Parameters vs arguments: the names in the method definition — a and b — are parameters. The real values you pass in when calling, like 5 and 3, are arguments.

blocks.heading: Methods that return vs methods that say void
blocks.html: Some methods hand back an answer with return — like add returning an int. Others just do something (like printing) and give nothing back. Those use the return type void, which means "no answer."

blocks.caption: A void method (greet) and a returning method (isEven)
blocks.output:
Hello, Maya!
Hello, Leo!
Is 4 even? true
Is 7 even? false
blocks.explain: greet returns void — it just prints, so we call it on its own line. isEven returns a boolean (true or false), so we can print its answer. % gives the remainder, so n % 2 == 0 is true when n divides evenly by 2.

blocks.heading: Method overloading: same name, different jobs 🪄
blocks.html: Java lets you have two methods with the same name as long as their parameters are different. This is called overloading. Java picks the right one by looking at the arguments you pass.

blocks.caption: Two add methods with the same name
blocks.explain: Both methods are called add, but one takes two numbers and one takes three. Java sees how many arguments you gave and runs the matching version. That is overloading.

blocks.html: Why bother with methods? They stop repetition. Write the logic once, name it, and reuse it everywhere. If you need to fix a bug, you fix it in one place instead of ten.

blocks.question: In the line static int add(int a, int b), what does the first int mean?
options: The return type — the kind of answer the method gives back
blocks.explain: The type written before the method name is the return type. Here it is int, so add hands back a whole number.

blocks.question: What return type do you use for a method that prints something but gives no value back?
blocks.explain: void means 'no answer.' Methods that just do an action (like printing) and return nothing use void.

blocks.question: Why is method overloading allowed for these two methods: add(int a, int b) and add(int a, int b, int c)?
options: Because they have the same name
Because they have different parameters (two numbers vs three)
Because Java allows any duplicate names
It is not allowed — this is an error
blocks.explain: Overloading needs the same name but DIFFERENT parameters. Here one takes two ints and the other takes three, so Java can tell them apart.

blocks.title: Write a square method
blocks.instructions: Finish the method square(int n) so it returns n multiplied by itself. Then it will be called from main. Press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Nice — your method squares any number you give it!
blocks.failMsg: Inside square, return n * n so it multiplies the number by itself.

blocks.title: Area of a circle
blocks.instructions: Complete areaOfCircle(double r) so it returns 3.14 times r times r. main will call it with 2.0. Press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Great — you turned a math formula into a reusable method!
blocks.failMsg: Return 3.14 * r * r so it multiplies pi by the radius twice.

challenge.title: Build a tiny method toolbox
challenge.instructions: Write a void method greet(String name) that prints "Hi, " then the name then "!", and an int method add(int a, int b) that returns the sum. In main, call greet("Sam") and then print add(8, 6). Press Check.
challenge.hint: greet uses void and just prints. add uses int and returns a + b. In main, call greet("Sam"); on its own line, then System.out.println(add(8, 6));
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You built your own toolbox of methods — that is exactly how real programs stay tidy. Day 10 complete!
challenge.failMsg: Make sure you have a static void greet(String name) method and a static int add(int a, int b) method, then call both from main.
